## [00:00 - 03:02]

Okay, so finally we're going to just end up with some product research tools. I already covered some of these earlier in the course, but you can never have too many tools for researching, at least if you're a research geek like me. And basically the Google keyword tool, Word Tracker, and MicroNiche Finder, and Market Samurai. A lot of people love Market Samurai. Getting it done tools. So these are great tools if you are working with other people and collaborating with other people. For instance, when I create a monster project like Product Launch Formula, that, you know, I mean it's gone through many iterations. The first iteration was me just doing it as a series of tele-seminars, basically. I think it was six tele-seminars. And what we did, or I did, was I think I sold for $2,000, and I just did it on a conference call line. Each call, before the call, I would send them out a Word document, or a PDF, I think. So I'm out of PDF, and then I would basically present from that PDF. And there's stuff they could fill in on the PDF, but basically I was presenting from a PDF. These days I would do it as a webinar. But back then, in what was that, 2005, I guess webinars probably were viable, but it was cutting edge at that point. So I did it as a tele-seminar with basically handouts. Then I went from there, and I created a home study course. Took basically what, I learned how to teach it in doing those tele-seminars, and basically taking questions on every phone call, and then basically doing surveys after each phone call to find out what worked and what I needed to do better, and what questions people had. I basically learned how to teach it. So I took that. I didn't actually use any of the recordings from the tele-seminars, but basically I took the learning I had, the learning of how to teach it. I knew all about product launches, but I had never taught it, so I wasn't sure how to teach it. So I learned to teach it through those tele-seminars. Then I created a home study course. Home study course, obviously the tele-seminar was that whole thing. I did 100% by myself. Tele-seminars, I'm sorry though, the home study course basically was all screen capture and audio. I did that pretty much all by myself. I did have some interviews where I used tele-conference lines to do those interviews and record those interviews. But then when I got to Product Launch Formula 2.0, we really grew up. I mean, it was a huge change, and all of a sudden I was using someone else to help me with some of the uploading of the product and getting the membership site set up.

## [03:02 - 06:03]

And then Product Launch Formula 3.0 was this quantum leap, and all of a sudden I had like a full team, and the team was helping me with graphics. They were helping me with editing. They were helping me with editing audio and video. They were getting stuff uploaded. They were creating action guides. There's a whole bunch of collaboration going on. I was doing all the 98% of the major product creation, actually 100% of that. But there's all extra pieces being added in. I have people out looking for these little clip art. I have people creating presentations for me. I have people creating graphics. I have people creating the membership site. So a lot of people helping edit audio and video. So lots of moving parts involved with that. And once you get to that level, maybe not for your first product, if you're just doing an e-book or something, but once you get to the level where you work with other people, now you need tools to collaborate with other people. So stuff we would use would be Google Docs. So Google Docs, you can create spreadsheets. You can create Word documents. You can create presentation files and basically share those. Multiple people can be working on them. And you have to be a little careful because if you have multiple people working on a Google document at the same time, it's possible to overwrite each other's changes and then the losing work. But if you're careful and the only one person is working on it at a time, I could check in like create a Word document. Maybe it's an action guide. And then I could have one of my team members go in and format it, change all the formatting, make it look good. And then another team member log on and do a proof it and get, you know, fix all the typos and the misspellings. And so Google Documents, you can be very, very powerful for creating documents. Basecamp and Central Desktop. And there's a whole bunch of others like huddle.net. They fall under what they call collaboration software. And that's so you can put tasks up and people can see what tasks need to be done. You can assign tasks and you can have a calendar to see when people should have this done or that done. You can share documents. Lots of stuff you can do on Basecamp and Central Desktop. Lots of people love this. Basecamp is by far the, I'm just guessing, but from anecdotally, it seems like it's by far the most popular. Lots of people really love that. If you Google Basecamp, I think it's available from 37signals.com. I'm going to go ahead and just type that in here. 37signals.com. Skype. Great way to stay in touch. You know, my team, we do, in addition to the collaboration software, you know, we're emailing back and forth from our communicating via email. But in addition, Skype, like, or any instant messaging service can be really great. So you know, you can use Google. You can use Yahoo, Instant Messenger, Skype, whatever. You know, there's a bunch of them.

## [06:03 - 09:04]

But it's a great way if you're, you know, really in the midst of creating a product, you know, you're working on this, that, you know, there's a lot of moving pieces for these bigger products. Instead of emailing back and forth, Skype, Instant Messenger, or any Instant Messenger, can be really handy. And nowadays, most of them will let you do audio and some of them will let you do video and they'll let you do file transfers. So it can be really useful for that. Doing conference calls to get everyone on the same page, freeconferencecall.com, again, works, works well for that. And something that's sort of, it's changed the way I work is Dropbox. And you can get that at getdropbox.com. And this is basically, it's a way you can share files with other people. But that's just one of the functions. Another function, it basically creates another folder on your machine or a directory on your machine if you're using Windows. So it's like, you have this Dropbox directory or a Dropbox folder. And everything you put into that Dropbox folder is instantly uploaded to Dropbox. And then any other machine that you have Dropbox on, it's instantly downloaded to that machine. So right now, I'm working on this big fancy Mac Pro desktop machine, but I also have a Macbook Pro notebook and a Mac Air notebook. And if I'm working on my Mac Pro here on my desktop machine, and I save something to my Dropbox file, it's basically uploaded instantly to Dropbox, which is in the cloud, and then instantly downloaded to those other machines. So like this project that I'm working on, this product creation product, I have it all in my Dropbox, which is just another folder on my machine. But that means I can work on it no matter what machine I'm on, because it's synced across all my machines instantly. In addition, I can create a folder within Dropbox that I share with my team. And so then as soon as something is done and uploaded to that folder, to my shared folder, then all my team members can get it on their desktop as well. So Dropbox is really changed. It's one of those tools that's literally changed the way I work on a daily basis, and it's also changed the way my team has worked. So just check it out. It's free. There's a paid version and a free version. A free version gets you a certain amount of storage. The paid version gets you more storage. It's an awesome tool. I love it. So those are just some tools that will help you get your projects done a lot faster if you're working with other people. Like I said, initially, you're probably just going to be working, you know, the way most people start off is working by themselves. And maybe they're writing an e-book or creating an audio product or a quick and dirty video product or whatever. But eventually, as your business grows and your success as a product creator and a publisher grow, you're going to want to create a team and build a team. And you know, in that case, these tools will really help in working with that team.
